Why does the storm have a name already when it's not really developed

it says "will likely become" - so it doesn't have a name "officially", but since it's predicted to develop over the weekend and there's nothing else likely to develop before it, they're just using the name to identify it.

I just don't remember them doing that in the past until it actually became a tropical storm at least. People just glancing in hearing a named storm will tend to think that's a hurricane when there isn't
IMO, its to push the agenda, so they can say: "more named storms this year", proving climate change.
if I'm not mistaken, they have the names A through Z every season on deck and ready to assign
right but it's not developed yet so I don't know why it's named I don't remember them doing that in me fast until it actually becomes a hurricane or at least a tropical storm
It's still not named, one day later. It gets it's name when it has a closed center of circulation and winds are 39 mph or higher.

what I'm saying is the name Debby is being used to identify the system even though it's not yet even a tropical storm, when in the past I don't recall this being done unless it was at least a tropical storm
winds of 74 or higher to be a hurricane
